text: Do you naturally write longer, multi-clause sentences or shorter ones?

options.label: Longer, multi-clause sentences — I let ideas flow in connected streams
Shorter sentences — I keep each thought tight and separate
A mix of both, depending on the moment

metadata.bias_mitigation: Both sentence lengths framed as natural stylistic preferences

text: Complex idea: break into multiple sentences or one elaborate sentence?

options.label: Break it into multiple shorter sentences for clarity
Craft one elaborate sentence that captures the full complexity
It depends on the idea and context

metadata.bias_mitigation: Both approaches framed as valid strategies for handling complexity

text: Do you front-load important information or build up to it?

options.label: Front-load — I lead with the key point and then elaborate
Build up — I set context and arrive at the main point
It varies depending on the situation

metadata.bias_mitigation: Both information ordering strategies presented as effective rhetorical choices

text: Which feels more natural? "The report was completed by the team" or "The team completed the report."

options.label: "The team completed the report" — active voice feels more natural
"The report was completed by the team" — passive voice feels more natural
Either works — I switch between them freely

metadata.bias_mitigation: Concrete example avoids abstract preference; both voices framed as natural choices

text: Do you write long paragraphs or shorter, punchier ones?

options.label: Very short and punchy — often just 1-2 sentences
Mostly short with occasional longer ones
A moderate mix
Somewhat long — I develop ideas before moving on
Moderately long — I build full arguments in each paragraph
Quite long — paragraphs are extended, detailed units
Very long — I write dense, elaborate paragraphs

metadata.bias_mitigation: Full 7-point scale with balanced endpoints; no length framed as superior

text: When starting a paragraph, lead with main point or build up?

options.label: Lead with the main point — topic sentence first, then support
Build up — set the stage before stating the point
It varies by paragraph and context

metadata.bias_mitigation: Both paragraph opening strategies presented as valid structural choices

text: How often do you use bulleted or numbered lists?

options.label: Almost never — I prefer continuous prose
Rarely — only when absolutely necessary
Sometimes — when the content calls for it
Often — lists are a regular part of my writing
Very frequently — I reach for lists whenever possible

metadata.bias_mitigation: Reversed scoring — frequent list use maps to lower complexity; both preferences normalized

options.label: Headings and subheadings extensively — I structure with clear signposts
Continuous prose — I let the writing flow without imposed structure
A moderate amount — headings for major sections but prose within them

metadata.bias_mitigation: Both formatting approaches framed as deliberate structural decisions

text: Do you elaborate extensively or express concisely?

options.label: Extremely concise — I strip away everything non-essential
Quite concise — brevity is a priority
Lean but with some elaboration where needed
Balanced between conciseness and elaboration
Moderately elaborate — I develop ideas with supporting detail
Quite elaborate — I explore ideas thoroughly
Extensively elaborate — I leave no angle unexplored

metadata.bias_mitigation: Full 7-point scale with balanced endpoints; neither extreme framed as superior

text: Organizing a piece: start with big picture or build from details?

options.label: Start with the big picture — outline the whole, then fill in details
Build from details — work through specifics and let the structure emerge
A combination — I move between big picture and details

metadata.bias_mitigation: Both organizational approaches framed as valid cognitive strategies
